title: Swansson to Ferrari, Cachinho to Haas for Bahrain and Jeddah in Temporary Swap
meta: Published: 09 April 2026 | By Isabella Rossi, Breaking News & Live Reporter | F1CC News
caption: Ferrari and Haas have agreed a two‑race driver exchange.
body:
LONDON — In the most dramatic driver market intervention of the 2026 season to date, Ferrari and Haas have agreed to a two‑race driver exchange that will see Riley Swansson replace Afonso Cachinho at the Scuderia for the upcoming Bahrain and Saudi Arabian Grands Prix. Cachinho will move in the opposite direction, partnering George Konstantaras at Haas for the same period.

The decision, confirmed by both teams on Thursday morning and effective immediately, is a direct response to Cachinho’s disastrous start to life in scarlet. The Portuguese driver has failed to score a single point across the opening four rounds, has accumulated six penalty points on his FIA racing licence — including a formal warning — and has been eliminated in Q1 at three of the four weekends. With Ferrari clinging to a 14‑point lead in the constructors’ championship thanks almost entirely to Eetu Väisänen’s 64‑point haul, the team could no longer afford to carry a second car that had contributed precisely nothing to the title fight.

Swansson, by contrast, has quietly excelled in a Haas that offers little margin for error. The Australian has scored two points — a 15th in Australia and a 9th in China — and has consistently dragged his car into positions it had no right to occupy. It is that resilience, and the steady hand he has shown under pressure, that has earned him a shot in the championship‑leading machine. For a driver who began the season as an afterthought, the call‑up represents a life‑changing opportunity — and a two‑race audition that could reshape his entire career.

The swap is strictly temporary, covering only the Bahrain Grand Prix this weekend and the Saudi Arabian Grand Prix the following week. But its implications stretch far beyond those two races. If Swansson can deliver points — or, better still, a podium — Ferrari will face an immediate dilemma: do they revert to Cachinho for the European leg, or do they extend the swap, or even consider a permanent change? The team has been careful not to frame the decision as a sacking, but Cachinho’s seat is now demonstrably under review. A strong showing from Swansson would turn that review into a crisis for the incumbent.

Cachinho’s fall from grace has been rapid and painful. When he was promoted from Haas in the off‑season to replace the retired Liam Dela Cruz, he was hailed as a natural fit for Ferrari’s long‑term project. Four races later, he is the only driver on the grid with zero points from a full‑time seat. His qualifying performances have been the most visible symptom: three Q1 exits — in Australia, China, and Japan — have forced him into recovery drives that his racecraft could not salvage. A late‑race collision in China added a fifth‑second penalty and two more penalty points to his record, bringing him within three points of an automatic race ban. For a driver already struggling with confidence, the pressure inside the Ferrari garage had become unbearable. A temporary move to Haas, where expectations are lower and the scrutiny less intense, may be the only way to reset.

For Haas, the arrangement offers a curious proposition. Cachinho arrives with far more pedigree than his results suggest, and in a car that demands less of its driver on the absolute limit, he may rediscover the form that once made him a Ferrari protégé. Haas have nothing to lose: their 4 points already place them last in the constructors’ standings, and a revitalised Cachinho could help them close the gap to the midfield. If he fails, they simply revert to their original lineup. The risk is minimal; the potential upside, if Cachinho finds his feet, is significant.

The broader driver market will also be watching closely. Ferrari’s academy pipeline includes two drivers currently competing in Formula 2 — Kanata Rivington at Hitech and Marcos Olmedo at Campos — both of whom will see the Swansson opportunity as evidence that seats can be pried open with the right combination of results and circumstance. If Swansson impresses and Cachinho continues to struggle, the summer silly season could arrive early, with Ferrari’s second cockpit at the centre of the storm.

Championship leader Eetu Väisänen, who will now partner Swansson in Bahrain, offered a measured response. "Yeah, I like Afonso and I do hope the best for him," he said. "But I stand by the team's decision."

The Bahrain Grand Prix takes place on 12 April at the Sakhir International Circuit. Both Swansson and Cachinho will be expected to participate in practice sessions on Friday, with qualifying on Saturday and the race under the floodlights on Saturday evening. For one driver, it is the opportunity of a lifetime. For the other, it is a chance to prove that his Ferrari career is not yet over. Both have exactly two races to make their case.

What it means: Ferrari have acted decisively to protect their constructors’ lead. Swansson gets an unexpected chance to stake a claim for a permanent seat. Cachinho gets a temporary escape from the pressure. The driver market has just been thrown wide open.

Last updated: 10:15 BST, 09 April 2026

[More to follow]
